coredata.dc:description: Ultra-small histidine-functionalized carbon nanodots (His C-dots) with engineered modality as photo-oxidative nanozymes for cancer theranostics were prepared by one step microwave-assisted pyrolysis. The role of chemically embedded imidazole ring into nanoparticle aromatic domains was to enhance the absorbance, fluorescence, and phosphorescence intensities in aqueous solution at room temperature. It contributed to the increase of pyrrolic nitrogen, which was responsible for the higher oxygen adsorption and better photosensitization under visible irradiation. The ability of nanozyme as theranostics was demonstrated on illuminated with visible light B16-F10 mouse melanoma cells with high metastatic potential under normoxic condition. The inhibition of cell migration was measured by wound healing assay after scratching confluent monolayer of the breast cancer cells. The microscopic data revealed that the metastatic migration could be controlled by the applied nanozyme dosage, which was responsible for the production of singlet oxygen and the causing of effective photo-induced cytotoxicity. The biocompatibility of the illuminated His C-dots was proved on ATPase activity of intact mitochondria and sub-mitochondrial particles, on diamine oxidase activity of liver and kidney fractions in dark mode, MTT assay as well as on non-illuminated adenocarcinomic human alveolar basal epithelial cells (A549) and human liver cancer cells (HepG2). The satisfactory ability of the reported nanoparticles as fluorescence anticancer agents makes them a promising platform for the development of oxidize-mimicking nanozymes with application in the photodynamic therapy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of the nanoparticles size distribution. Transmission electron microscopic micrographs of: (A) the granularity of ultra-thin carbon film as a substrate, and (B) histidine-functionalized carbon nanodots on the carbon film. (C) Scanning electron microscopy image of the nanozyme. (D) Dynamic light scattering profile of nanoparticles size distribution is suspension. Scale bars for A and B = 20 nm, for C = 10 nm.

Chemical analysis of the nanozyme. Vibrational (A) Raman spectrum of nanozyme (upside, blue dashed line) and bare C-dots (downside, red solid line) and (B) FTIR spectrum of the nanoparticles. XPS spectra of (C) 40 % histidine-derived carbon nanodots (His C-dots), and (D) histidine-free carbon nanodots as a control experiment.

Optical properties of the nanozyme. (A) Jablonski diagram indication of the electronic state and their relative energy levels and the production of singlet oxygen. (B) The bathochromic absorption effect of the nanozyme (solid red line) with peak maximum at 353 nm and its histidine-free counterpart as control experiment (dashed blue line) with peak maximum at 360 nm. (C) Down-conversion (solid blue line) and upconversion (solid red line) fluorescence of the nanozyme, excited with 400 and 700 nm irradiation, respectively. The peaks maximums are at 463 and 605 nm. The down-conversion (dashed green line) and upconversion (dashed orange line) fluorescence intensities of the histidine-free carbon dots are significantly lower. (D) Same effect is demonstrated with the down-conversion and upconversion phosphorescence, where the maximum peaks of the nanozyme are located at 453 nm (blue solid line) and around 600 nm (red solid line). The down-conversion fluorescence intensity of control experiment is (0 % His, green dashed line) is lower and the upconversion intensity (dashed orange line) is practically missing.

Confocal microscopy images of melanoma B16-F10 breast cancer cells. (A) Bright field contrast image of untreated cancer cells. (B) Epifluorescence image of cells stained with nanozyme. (C) Confocal microscopy image revealing the intracellular uptake of nanoparticles based on their one-photon fluorescence. (D) A bright-field contrast image of cancer cells treated with nanozyme. (E) Two-photon excited fluorescence imaging of the stained cells irradiated with 700 nm NIR. (F) Merged micrograph showing the nanoparticle distribution in the intact cells based on the upconversion fluorescence. Scale bars for A, B, C = 50 μm, for D, E and F = 20 μm.

Oxygen photosensitization by the nanozyme. (A) Absorption spectra of oxidized TMB (presented with reaction at the peak of 645 nm) by the nanozyme at neutral pH. The oxidation of histidine group and formation of carbonyls is schematically presented at the upper side (next to the peak of 355 nm). (B) Time-dependent absorbance changes at 645 nm as a result of the catalyzed oxidation of TMB by the nanozyme (red color) and histidine-free C-dots (blue color, control experiment). (C) Characterized singlet oxygen phosphorescence emission spectra of the nanozyme in CD3CN-D2O mixed solvent. (D) EPR analysis of the photosensitized generation of 1O2 by TEMP indication.

In vitro scratch assay conducted on tumor melanoma B16-F10 in the absence (–) and presence (+) of the nanozyme under normoxia condition. The cell migration was induced by scratching cellular monolayer of cells with a plastic pipette tip. (Upper side fluorescent micrographs) B16F10 cell density is confluent in the culture dish at the initial point of the experiment. Then, wound lines were formed by scratching the cellular monolayer. The width of scratch was recorded 24 h continuously by periodically taking photographs under inverted microscope. The cellular occupation of the empty area in each experiment was quantified by ImageJ analysis software. (Down-side) The percentage of melanoma cell migration is represented by a bar graph indicating the experimentally measured scopes of scratch assay data.

Melanoma B16-F10 cancer cells were incubated in the culture dishes until reaching of the confluent cell density under normoxia condition in RPMI 1640 complete medium. After that the cells were washed and treated for one hour with nutrition medium and nanozyme with doses 0.0, 0.5, 5.0, 25.0 and 50.0 μg/ml, respectively. After that wound lines were made by scratching cellular monolayer with a plastic pipette tip. The culture dishes were initially illuminated with LED lamp to activate the photo-oxidase nanozyme, and the wound areas were imaged at 24 h post-scratching period. The change of scratch width was recorded by taking photographs under phase contrast microscope. The empty area in each experiment was quantified by ImageJ software. The percentage of cell migration across the wound regions was reduced as a function of the applied raising applied dosage as follow: 69, 62, 48, 27 and 14 %, respectively. This indicate that the nanozyme have ability to inhibit B16-F10 cell migration under light irradiation, i.e. photodynamic therapy.

In vitro photo-induced cytotoxicity of nanozyme of six different nanozyme doses (0.5, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0 25.0 and 50.0 μg/ml) at dark (0 min, control experiment) and irradiation time within 10 min (blue line), 20 min (violet line) and 30 min (red line). Quantities represents ± SEM (p ≤ 0.001).

Toxicity assessment on mitochondrial and sub-mitochondrial particles. (A) Effect of His C-dots on ATPase activity of intact liver mitochondria. After pre-incubating the mitochondrial suspension (protein 5.86 mg/sample) and nanozyme Nz doses of 50, 165 and 500 mg/l (from left to right) in the assay medium for 10 min the ATPase reaction was started by adding of ATP (final concentration of 1 mM) and carried out for 5 min. Curves present data from single experiment with Nz. DNP is 2,4-dinitrophenol. (B) Effect of His C-dots on ATPase activity of SMP. ATPase activity were calculated as percentages of the activity measured under control conditions (nanozyme-free assay media, C). Data are plotted as mean ± SEM of six independent experiments (three parallel samples per group per experiment). Asterisks indicate significant differences (*p < 0.05; **p < 0.01) from the control.

Effect of nanozyme on DAO activity of rat liver and kidney in vitro (A) and in vivo (B) experiments. DAO activity values were calculated as percentages of the activity measured under control conditions (nanozyme free assay media). Data are plotted as mean ± SEM of five independent experiments (three parallel samples per group per experiment). Effects of the nanozyme are not a statistically significant from the control.
Growth curves of control cells and treated for 6 h with 350 mg/l His C-dots. a) A549 cells; b) HepG2 cells. Data are represented as mean ± SE, n = 3.
Bright field image of treated with C-Dots and untreated A549 and HepG2 six days after growing on the plate. The scale bar = 10 ϻm.

head.ce:title: Photo-oxidase carbon dot-based nanozyme for breast cancer theranostics under normoxia condition

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultra-small histidine-functionalized carbon nanodots (His C-dots) with engineered modality as photo-oxidative nanozymes for cancer theranostics were prepared by one step microwave-assisted pyrolysis. The role of chemically embedded imidazole ring into nanoparticle aromatic domains was to enhance the absorbance, fluorescence, and phosphorescence intensities in aqueous solution at room temperature. It contributed to the increase of pyrrolic nitrogen, which was responsible for the higher oxygen adsorption and better photosensitization under visible irradiation. The ability of nanozyme as theranostics was demonstrated on illuminated with visible light B16-F10 mouse melanoma cells with high metastatic potential under normoxic condition. The inhibition of cell migration was measured by wound healing assay after scratching confluent monolayer of the breast cancer cells. The microscopic data revealed that the metastatic migration could be controlled by the applied nanozyme dosage, which was responsible for the production of singlet oxygen and the causing of effective photo-induced cytotoxicity. The biocompatibility of the illuminated His C-dots was proved on ATPase activity of intact mitochondria and sub-mitochondrial particles, on diamine oxidase activity of liver and kidney fractions in dark mode, MTT assay as well as on non-illuminated adenocarcinomic human alveolar basal epithelial cells (A549) and human liver cancer cells (HepG2). The satisfactory ability of the reported nanoparticles as fluorescence anticancer agents makes them a promising platform for the development of oxidize-mimicking nanozymes with application in the photodynamic therapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: During the last decade, the nanomaterial-based enzyme mimics (nanozymes) were extensively developed as an emerging platform for cancer theranostics [1–3]. Among them the novel class of ultra-small carbon nanoparticles (carbon nanodots or C-dots) attracted close attention because of their outstanding advantages, such as unique photophysical properties for bio-imaging and antineoplastic application, robust chemical inertness, low toxicity and strong resistance against degradation in high-stress environment, as well as because of their potential for design of anticancer drug delivery nanosystems [4–6]. C-dots are characterized with a hydrophobic graphitic core obtained by incomplete carbonization, which is decorated by hydrophilic surface chemical groups, such as alkyl, carboxyl, amino, hydroxyl and carbonyl moieties and thus might mimic the structure and functions of natural enzymes [7,8]. Interestingly, C-dots are proved to bypass the complex tumor microenvironmental barriers, as well as high penetration of the blood brain barrier [9]. They have also demonstrated an enhanced permeability effect and tend to accumulate inside solid tumors much more than they do in normal tissue due to their small sizes and being organic in nature [10]. This ability enables them a promising advantage and a viable option to improve the efficacy of the noninvasive cancer chemotherapy [11]. Therefore, C-dots are harnessed in various nanotheranostic strategies for diverse tumors through integrating with other functional biomaterials or utilizing their inherent physical–chemical properties [36–38]. In that respect, numerous functional ligands (e.g. folic acid, transferrin, aptamers etc.) might be conjugated to these organic groups to achieve specific identification and active targeting to cancer cells through recognition reaction with their over-expressed membrane receptor clusters [12,39]. The quantitative cellular accumulation of C-dots with attached targeting ligands on cancerous cells and their healthy counterpart demonstrated more efficient uptake in cancerous cells than in healthy cells [13,14]. Such nanoparticles are able to accumulate in the metastatic cells via effective receptor-mediated endocytosis [15]. Many in vitro studies confirmed the internalization of C-dots not only in the cancer cells cytoplasm, but also in their mitochondria [16,17]. However, additional modification of C-dots aimed enhancing the oxygen photosensitization capability under visual or near infrared light is necessary to provide the opportunity to expand their applications as anti-cancer drugs for photodynamic therapy.

The aim of current report is to synthesize semiconductive carbon nanodots with engineered bandgap, surface and catalytical properties, which act as photo-oxidase nanozyme for breast cancer theranostic under normoxia condition. For that purpose, the nitrogen-doped C-dots with chemically embedded imidazole rings were developed as enzyme-mimicking nanomaterials with enhanced adsorption capacity to molecular oxygen and improved photosensitization performance. This is because the introduced pyrrolic nitrogen in the aromatic lattice functions as a major O2 adsorption site and thus enables to shorten the distance between the hydrophobic domain and the oxygen molecule [18]. This effect is a crucial step for improvement of the catalytic photosensitization reaction. Thus, the designed theranostics C-dots might act as photo-oxidase enzyme mimics, i.e. nanozymes, to produce singlet oxygen (1O2) upon visible light irradiation and further to generate a series of energy transfer reactions from the triplet state leading to the accumulation of cytotoxic singlet oxygen or reactive oxygen species (ROS). The generated ROS affect the mitochondrial viability in the cancer cells, induce apoptosis and promote DNA fragmentation, which results in irreversible programmed death [19]. The cytotoxicity of photo-oxidase nanozyme was demonstrated on human breast cancer cells B16F10 by measuring their mobility through an in vitro scratch assay. The inhibition of cell migration might be used to assess the anticancer nanozyme photo-oxidase activity. The results demonstrated that under exposure to visible light, the nanozyme dose dependently mediated photo-induced cytotoxicity on the metastatic mobility. Further, the biocompatibility of the nanozyme at dark mode was demonstrated on ATPase activity of intact mitochondria and submitochondrial particles (SMP), as well as on diamine oxidase (DAO) activity of liver and kidney fractions. The cytotoxicity and the ability of C-dots to penetrate human cell lines A549 (adenocarcinomic human alveolar basal epithelial cells) and HepG2 (human liver cancer cells) were studied using cell growth experiments and bright field microscopy visualization. Both cell lines are suitable for research of microsomal fractions and vesicular traffic by endocytic processes [20].

Deionized water obtained from a Milli-Q device (Japan Millipore ltd., Tokyo, Japan) was used in all experiments. Citric acid, 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA), l -histidine amino acid, 2,2,6,6- tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA) and manganese sulfate pentahydrate were of analytical-reagent grade, purchased from Wako, Japan and used without further purification for the synthesis of carbon nanoparticles. 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and dissolved in dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) to obtain a freshly prepared stock solution. Sucrose, KCl, MgCl2, KH2PO4, H2SO4, sodium deoxycholate, FeSO4, ammonium molibdate (H2SO4(NH4)6Mo7O24) and Folin’s reagent were purchased from Merck (Germany); ATP, Tris, 2,4-dinitrophenol (DNP) and all other salts were obtained from Sigma Aldrich (Saint Louis, USA); perchloric acid (HClO4) – from Riedel de Haën (Germany). Fresh stock solution of C-dots was prepared prior to each experiment. The nanoparticle dispersions were homogenized by an ultrasonic bath UST2.4–150 (Siel, Gabrovo, Bulgaria) for 30 min prior to each experiment. The purified nanozyme was mixed with a filtered d-phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solution, which contains 2.7 mM KCl, 1.5 mM KH2PO4, 140 mM NaCl, 3.2 mM NaH2PO4.12 H2O. The photo-oxidase activity of the nanoparticles were tested in a photocatalytic cell equipped with a LED lamp, glass filter (400–700 nm), quartz cuvette and a stand for placing the cuvettes. The cultivation of melanoma cancer cells and the scratch test assay was performed in an incubator, which was additionally equipped with LED lamp, glass filter and inverted microscope (Eclipse Ti, Nikon, Japan). The fluorescent yield of histidine-free C-dots and nanozyme was calculated as follow [35]: Sodium fluorescein was selected as a reference material. Small amount of the nanoparticles and sodium fluorescein were diluted and their fluorescence emission spectra were measured at excitation wavelength 350 nm. The fluorescence quantum yield was calculated from the following equation:

where ∅ is the quantum yield, the subscripts c and s represents nanoparticles and sodium fluorescein, respectively. I – integral area of the fluorescence emission spectrum, A – UV absorbance at 350 nm, η– the amount of the solvent refractive index.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and photophysical characterization of histidine-derived nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: The nanoparticles were synthesized via one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis [21]. For that purpose, different ratio of citric acid to histidine (i.e. 10 %, 20 %, 30 %, 40 % and 50 % histidine) was tested in respect to obtain carbon nanodots with highest absorption, fluorescence, and phosphorescence intensity. It was found experimentally that the optimum ratio between citric acid and l -histidine was 3:2. So, 600 mg citric acid (main source of carbon) and 400 mg l -histidine (main source of pyrrolic N) were dissolved in 10 ml ultrapure deionized water (dH2O) under vigorous mixing. After formation of a transparent solution 0.2 ml of EDA (main source of graphitic, pyridinic and amine N) was injected to the reaction mixture. The color of precursors solution was immediately turn to light yellow. Then, the dissolved precursors were subject of microwave-assisted pyrolysis through heating in a microwave oven (700 W) for around 2 min using rotating plate. After cooling down to ambient temperature, the resulted yellowish brown solid was kept under redused pressure for 60 min to remove the volatile organic compounds, which included also unreacted precursors. The obtained gum was dispersed again into 10 ml dH2O to extract the water-soluble nanoparticles in the aqueous phase. The supernatant was centrifugated (14 000 rpm) to remove the haevier particles, and finally, the carbon dots were separated from the solts and other byproducts by size-selective desalting column (PD-10 desalting column, GE Healthcare Life Sciences). The purity of isolated nanoparticles was confirmed by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). The purified nanoparticles were observed in scanning transmission electron microscope (STEM, JEOL 2010F) operated at 200 kV accelerating voltage and the average nanoparticle hydrodynamic diameter was confirmed by dynamic light scattering (DLS, Brookhaven Instrument Corporation, model No. BI-200SM). The optical properies of nanoparticles, i.e. absorbance, fluorescence, and phosphorescence were measured by UV–vis Jasco analytical spectrophotometer (model No. V-570), Hitachi fluorescence spectrophotometer (model N. F-7100), respectively. The phosphorescence was measured by FP-8300 spectrometer, excitation band 5 nm, emission band width 5 nm, period 100 msec, delay time 10 ms, integration time 65 msec, response 0.2 sec. Raman spectra were obtained by a Laser Raman WiTec Alpha 300 spectrometer through green exciting radiation (532 nm) from an Ar+ ion laser. FT-IR spectrum were taken by FT-IT spectrometer TENSOR II (Bruker) equipped with Platinum ATR. All optical measurements were done at ambient temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photosensitization reaction and singlet oxygen detection
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic ability of the histidine-derivatied C-dots (His C-dots) as a photooxidaze nanozyme was demonstrated quantitatively by oxidation of TMB in presence of ligand-stabilized Mn(III) with EDTA [22]. For that purpose, a stock solution was prepared by dissolving of 11.6 mg EDTA (1 × 103 M) and 36 mg MnSO4·5H2O (1 × 103 M) into 40 ml ddH2O (deionized distilled water). 7.7 mg of TMB were preliminary dissolved in DMSO and added into the stock solution. 3 ml of the stock solution were transfered into a qurtz quvette and nanoparticles (to form 30 mg/ml final concentration) were added to the reaction mixture. The quvette was irradiated with LED light (lamp KPF-2A, Japan) in the VIS range 400 – 700 nm (as described above) for certain intervals of time, and the blue color change was measured at 645 nm by PD-307S spectrophotometer (APEL, Japan). The phosphorescence spectra of generated singlet oxygen (1O2) was obtained by Fluorolog-3 spectrophotometer. The electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) measurements were conducted by 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) as spin traps for measurements of singlet oxygen levels. In brief, 5 μL of nanozyme aqeous solution (5 mg/ml) and 25 μL of TEMP (10 M) were mixed in 1 ml of buffer with pH 7. The mixture solution were illuminated by 365 nm (Handy UV Lamp, LUV-4, As One, Japan) for one minute before the EPR measurements (Bruker ELEXSYS E500 spectrometer).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culturing, treatment and measurement of cells migration under normoxia condition
ce:section.ce:para: B16 melanoma variant with low (B16-F1) and high (B16-F10) metastatic potential (RIKEN cell bank, Wako, Japan) were stored in refrigerator at – 80 °C and used in the study [23]. However, most experiments of photo-oxidase inhibition with the nanozyme were done on the B16-F10 variant. The cancer cells were grown in RPMI-1640 complete nutrition medium supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (Merk, Japan) and 1 % penicillin–streptomycin (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Japan). The microbial suspension was cultivated at 37 °C in the presence of 5 % CO2. After that the medium was removed by a Pasteur pipette and the adherent cells were washed by adding 5 ml 1 × sterile PBS solution (free of calcium and magnesium). For in vitro testing experiments, the cells were detached by adding 1 ml 1 × trypsin (the trypsinization process was confirmed by light microscope) and resuspended in RPMI-1640 nutrition medium supplemented with10 % FBS to obtain a diluted density of ∼ 2 × 105 cells/ml. A 9 ml portion of the obtained cell suspension was spread onto the substrates in a flask and maintained at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 to generate a monolayer. When B16-F10 cell density was confluent in the culture dish the monolayer was washed with PBS at room temperature. After that three wound lines in the form of a cross were made by scratching cellular monolayer with a plastic pipette tip. Then, floating cells were washed out. PBS was exchanged with fresh portion of medium (with or without nanozyme) and incubated for an hour under normoxia condition. Both samples (control experiment and treatment with nanozyme) were kept under irradiation with visible light (LED lamp) for a certain of time (e.g., 10 or 20 min) and the inhibition of cells migration was observed by microscope (Olympus SZX16, Japan). The used amounts of nanozyme were 50 μg/ml, 25 μg/ml, 5 μg/ml and 0.5 μg/ml, respectively. As the incubation time passed, the width of scratch was narrowed, and it was recorded by taking photographs under an inverted microscope [24]. The areas occupied by migrated bordering cells vs time was measured by ImageJ analysis software from the micrographic images. The collected experimental data were tested for statistical significance by Student’s t-test (p value of < 0.05).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxic effect of the nanozyme and viability analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Intact liver mitochondria and submitochondrial particles (SMPs) were isolated from male albino rats (Wistar strain; 50–60 days of age; 120–150 g) as previously described [24]. All studies were performed in accordance with the institutional ethical guidelines.

The cancer B16-F10 cells were seeded into 96-well plates in 100 μL nutrition medium and density of 1 × 104 cells/well. After 8 h of incubation (as described above), the medium was replaced with 100 μL fresh RPMI 1640 complete medium, which contained 0.5, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0 25.0 and 50.0 μg/ml nanozyme for each sample and was irradiated with LED lamp for 0, 10, 20 and 30 min, respectively. After 24 h incubation of the treated cells an aliquot of MTT solution (concentration 5 mg/ml in PBS) was added into each well. The treated cells were incubated again for 4 h at 37 °C and 5 % CO2. Then, the suspension of each well was dissolved by adding of 100 μL of a solubilizing solution for 20 min at ambient temperature. The absorbance intensity was measured at 570 nm using a SpectraMax® M2e instrument.

ATPase activity of mitochondria and SMP was determined by measurement of inorganic phosphate (Pi) released from ATP. The experimental conditions were as previously described [24] with modifications. The ATPase reaction with intact mitochondria was carried out at room temperature and continuous stirring in 5 ml assay medium consisting of 0.2 M sucrose, 10 mM KCl, 50 mM Tris-HCl, 100 μM EDTA-KOH (pH 7.5) and 50 µM DNP was included wherever indicated. Aliqouts of the CDs stock solution were added to reach final doses of 50, 165 and 500 mg/l respectively. After pre-incubating the mitochondria in the assay medium for 10 min the reaction was initiated by the addition of ATP (final concentration of 1 mM). Samples of 500 µL were taken after 30, 60, 120, 180 and 300 s incubation and added to 200 µL of 3 M perchloric acid for termination of the reaction. The activity of mitochondrial ATPase was expressed as µmol Pi.mg protein-1.

The reaction with the submitochondrial particles was carried out in 1 ml reaction medium consisting of 0.2 M sucrose, 10 mM KCl, 50 mM Tris-HCl, 100 μM EDTA-KOH and 1 mM MgCl2, pH 7.5. Aliquots of the CDs stock solution were added to reach final doses of 50, 165 and 500 mg/l respectively. After pre-incubating the SMP in the assay medium for 10 min at 37 °C the reaction was initiated by the addition of 1 mM ATP (final concentration), continued for 5 min and terminated by adding 0.4 ml of 3 M perchloric acid. The submitochondrial ATPase activity was expressed as µmol Pi.mg protein-1.min−1.

HepG2 (hepatocyte carcinoma) and A549 (lung carcinoma) cells were grown in DMEM F-12 Ham and in DMEM respectively with 10 % (v/v) FCS, streptomycin (0.1 mg/ml), penicillin (0.06 mg/ml) at 37 °C in 5 % CO2 water-saturated atmosphere. Both cell types were treated with 350 mg/l nanoparticles for 6 h. For growth curve experiments cells were plated at initial concentration of 5 x104 cells per well (for 24 well plates) and the cells were count (using camera of Burker) every 24 h up to seven days and were carried out by means of three independent wells for evaluation of the experimental errors. Bright field microscopy images and cell counting were carried out with Nikon Eclipse Ti-U (Nikon GmbH, Vienna) using 20x objective lens.

Female rats Wistar 4–6 weeks of age, average body weight (BW) 100 ± 20 g. The animals were kept under standard conditions at a room temperature of 22 ± 2 °C, 50–60 % humidity and 12:12 light–dark cycle. The rats were fasted 24 h and were allowed free access to water. The control-group animals were treated with 200 µL 0.9 % NaCl. C-nano particles were suspended in physiological saline (0.9 % NaCl) at doses of 165 mg/l (in vitro) and 4 mg/100 g body weight (in vivo). For the in vivo experiments, nanoparticles suspension of 200 µL were administrated intraperitoneal injections at a dose of 4 mg/100 g body weight. In in vitro experiments C-nano particles were apply in the samples before preincubation (see Assay of DAO activity). After 4 h, the animals of both groups were anesthetized by the inhalation of diethyl ether and decapitation. The liver and kidneys were immediately isolated, washed with cold sodium phosphate buffer 0.01 M, pH 7.0, weighed and homogenized in a ratio 1:4 (w:v) with same buffer. The homogenate was heated at 60° C in a water-bath for 10 min and centrifuged at 20 000g for 20 min. The supernatant was collected for analysis.

DAO activity was determined spectrophotometrically by the method of Hayashi et al. [25] as reported previously [26] using putrescine as substrate. Hydrogen peroxide, formed in the amine oxidase reaction, was measured photometrically (λ = 500 nm) by coupling 4-aminoantipirine with phenol in the presence of peroxidase. The standard reaction mixture for DAO activity assay (3.0 ml final volume), before the photometric measurement, contained 0.1 M sodium phosphate buffer (pH 7.4), 0.82 mM 4-aminoamtipirine. 10.6 mM phenol, 12 IU peroxidase, 2.5 mM putrescine, 1.0 mM semicarbazide and 300 µL supernatant. The enzyme assay was carried out as follows: the blanks, containing buffer, enzyme sours and peroxidase were preincubated with semicarbazide at 37 °C in the water bath for 20 min. The samples containing the same components except semicarbazide were also preincubated under the same conditions. The blanks and the samples were also contained C-nano particles (in vitro experiments) before preincubation. After preincubation added putrescine, 4-aminoantipirin, phenol and all tubs were incubated at 37 °C in the water bath for 60 min. The reaction was stopped by chilling the tubes on ice and semicarbazide was added to the samples. Protein content was determined by the method of Lowry [27] with using bovine serum albumin as a standard.

Data are expressed as mean ± standard error of the mean (SEM) of at least four independent experiments performed in three parallel samples. When appropriate, the difference between nanoparticles-treated samples and the untreated controls was tested by one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by Tukey test with treatment as a factor. The data were not normally distributed, and non-parametric Kruskall-Wallis One Way Analysis of Variance on Ranks test was performed. A value of p < 0.05 was considered significant. All statistical analyses were computed using SigmaStat version 3.5. The statistical significance of differences between two groups of DAO studies date were estimated by the Student‘s t-test. The values were considered significant when p < 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis and characterization of the nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: The size distribution and morphology of the synthesized histidine-functionalized carbon nanodots have been characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM), scanning transmission electron microscope (STEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) as shown on Fig. 1 . A high quality and low grained electron transparent carbon layer with homogeneous thickness of 15 nm was used as a support (Fig. 1A). The single nanoparticles absorbed on the carbon layer are then shadowed leading to higher contrasted TEM images (Fig. 1B). The observation revealed that carbon nanodots are highly monodisperse and relative uniform in size and shape. As illustrated, their shape is oval with average diameter between 3 and 4 nm. The smaller nanoparticles are difficult to distinguish from the granular background of the carbon film. Nevertheless, their presence was confirmed by atomic force microscope and dynamic light scattering (DLS).

The average nanoparticle diameter and size distribution was verified also with scanning transmission electron microscopic image (Fig. 1C) and dynamic light scattering profile (Fig. 1D). All DLS analytical data proved the formed average hydrodynamic diameter of 4 nm. Therefore, the nanozyme is considered as a kind of 0D nanomaterial, which consist of a carbon skeleton with abundant functional groups. Its vibrational spectroscopic data are accessed through Raman and FTIR analysis, as shown on Fig. 2 A and B, respectively. The broad and intense peaks in the Raman spectrum at 1382 cm−1 and 1568 cm−1 are ascribed to to the D (disorder) and G (graphite) bands (Fig. 2A). The detailed analysis and comparison of the Raman spectrum of bare C-dots (d-band = 1360 cm−1, G-band = 1570 cm−1) and nanozyme revealed a slight shifting of the d-band to longer wavelength with 22 cm−1. In the case of nanozyme there is also an enhanced overlapping between D and G peaks in the spectrum. The D band is related to the carbon virbations with dangling bonds in the disordered graphite. While the higher intense G band derives to E2g mode of the vibration of graphite sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagone lattice. The higher intensity indicates that the histidine-derived carbon nanodots are mainly composed of sp2 graphitic carbon core with sp3 carbon defects. The core structure consisted of both sp3 and sp2 – hybridized carbon atoms, which indicate the formation of amorphous carbon and graphite lattice, which is attributed to the nanoparticle’s carbonization degree. The functional groups as – NH2, CO, –OH, CN, COC, imidazole, etc., are attached to the graphitic-structured nanozyme during the microwave-assisted pyrolysis reaction, which can be proven by the FTIR spectra (Fig. 2B). The broad bands centered at 3300–3500 cm−1 and 3150 cm−1 in both spectra are attributed to –NH2 (or –NH3 +) and –OH groups, respectively. The band at 2940 cm−1 correspond to alkyl CH stretching. The presence of carboxylic groups (–COOH) is demonstrated by the stretching vibration at 1076 cm−1 and 1645 cm−1 with higher intensity at the histidine-free carbon nanodots (as control experiment). The peaks in the range of 1430–1550 cm−1 (downside red spectrum, 40 % His) are assigned to the vibration of the introduced imidazole into the carbon skeleton. They are missing in the control experiment (upside blue spectrum, 0 % His). The other bands around 1300 – 1410 cm−1 are identified as aromatic or aliphatic –CH2– groups. In conclusion, those large amount of polar surface groups give rise to the excellent water solubility of the nanoparticles. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XSP) analysis reveal the predominance of carbon (C 1 s, 285.0 eV), oxygen (O 1 s, 532.1 eV) and nitrogen (N 1 s, 400.1 eV) in the prepared carbon nanodots. The attention is focused particularly on N 1 s spectrum, which is demonstrated with pyridinic (red, 397.9–398.8 eV), pyrrolic (blue, 399.4–400.5 eV) and graphitic (green, 400.5–402.1 eV) nitrogen atoms. The graphitic N is a nitrogen atom attached to three carbon atoms in the graphite sheets, pyridinic N is a nitrogen atom attached between two carbon atoms at the edge of the sheet surface. The pyrrolic N is a nitrogen atom conjugated to a π bond system in the graphite sheets through two p electrons. As shown, the addition of imidazole from histidine-doping precursor (Fig. 2C) leads to significantly increases the amount of pyrrolic nitrogen in comparison with the histidine-free C-dots as a control experiment (Fig. 2D).

Since pyrrolic N was identified as the major adsorption site of O2, its higher contents in the designed strategy help to shorten the distance between the fluorescent nanoparticle and the dissolved oxygen molecules. This is a key factor for performance improvement of the catalytic photosensitization reaction and increasing the cytotoxicity of the nanozyme inside the cancer cells when they are subject of light irradiation. Obviously, the increased nitrogen doping into nanozyme’ lattice effects the energy levels and activation of its singlet and triplet states, and therefore is directly associated with the oxygen photosensitization reaction. Since the nanoparticles are so heterogeneous by structure and size, it is quite difficult to determine experimentally the precise exciting energy states in manner like a molecule. Although the diversity and large difference of the carbon nanodots, a proposed Jablonsky diagram is presented on Fig. 3 A based on theoretical calculations of simplified models of coronene and pyrene structures [29,32]. All calculations demonstrated that the energy values for the first singlet state (S1) are average between 2.20 ∼ 2.60 eV, while the values for the triplet state (T1) are between 1.60 ∼ 1.80 eV, which is dependent on their size and shape [28]. In all cases the presence of graphitic nitrogen is leads to the highest reduction of the difference between excited singlet states and excited triplet states, which is crucial to promote the intersystem crossing. Nevertheless, beside the triplet state activation, another crucial factor is the interaction between the nanozyme and dissolved oxygen. The simulation of adsorption process revealed that energy favorable in the case of structures doping with pyrrolic nitrogen. Thus, it is reasonable to accept that the enhanced oxygen adsorption by pyrrolic N leads to improved oxygen photosensitization reaction [29]. This was also proved experimentally, where the synthesized His C-dots demonstrated enhanced photo-oxidation activity from those without histidine. The experiments demonstrated also that the amount and variation of nitrogen speciation effect the UV–vis absorption spectra of nanoparticles (Fig. 3B). As shown, C-dots have a broad peak around 340–360 nm, which is assigned to n-π* transition of CN and CO chromophoric groups. The absorption tail with decreased intensity is observed in the visual range, which is formed from the hybrid structure between the aromatic core and related chemical groups. The increasing of pyrrolic N induced a slight blue shift from 360 nm (0 % His C-dots, as control experiment) to 353 nm (40 % His C-dots). The peak intensity increased significantly too, which is crucial for the improved fluorescence, phosphorescence, as well as the quantum yield. The calculated quantum yield of histidine-free C-dots was about 36.2 %, whereas the nanozyme quantum yield reaches about 51.3 %. The nanozyme possess slight red-shifted and excitation-dependent emission spectra as presented on Fig. S1. The increased intensity of both absorbance and fluorescence of the nanozyme in comparison to the bare C-dots (as control experiment) in aqueous solution might be seen even with naked eye, as show on the photographic image (Fig. S2). The nanoparticles emmision is comparatively stable against photobleaching under explosure to UV lights for hours. Interestingly, the nanozyme demonstrated significantly stonger resistance to photobleaching desrtuction in comparison to measured data of bare C-dots (Fig. S3). As shown on Fig. 2C, when excited with 400 nm the down-conversion fluorescence emission S1 → S0 of nanozyme (blue solid line, left axis) is at least an order of magnitude greater than those of histitidine-free carbon nanodots (green dashed line, left axis). Interestingly, such phenomenon is observed in the case of the upconversion fluorescence when the nanoparticles are excited with 700 nm near-infrared irradiation (NIR). The absorption peak is around 350, and therefore the maximum two-photon absorption cross section (TPACS) was measured exactly at 700 nm, where it must be theoretically expected [30]. The nanozyme is able to convert effectively the low-energy photons into higher-energy red light with wavelength maximum around 600–605 nm (red colored spectrum, right axis). The control experiment (0 % His C-dots, orange spectrum) show complete absence of any upconversion fluorescence. The difference between the maximum peaks of down-conversion and upconversion PL is about 140 nm (∼0.628 eV). Further, it is interesting to note that the nanozyme possessed both standard and upconversional phosphorescence (Fig. 3D). When excited with 400 nm UV the hystidine-contained nanoparticles exhibited sufficiently bright blue phosphorescence (solid blue line), which was significantly higher from their histidine-free counterpart (dashed green line). The measured lifetime of T1 → S0 was over 90 μs, which is 1 × 104 longer than that of the corresponding fluorescence S1 → S0 emission. The upconversion phosphorescence is comparatively lower in intensity (solid red line), but it is quite disapering in the control experiment (dashed orange line). The difference between the maximum peaks of down-conversion and upconversion phosphorescent is about 147 nm (∼0.67 eV). The role presence of graphitic nitrogen in the presented design also ensures a favorable intersystem crossing energy level (ΔEST), which is necessary for the easier activation of O2 triplet states into singlet one.

Nevertheless, all nanoparticles exhibit excitation-dependent emission in a wide visible wavelength range, which is due to the plentiful fluorophores, trap states, complex energy level transitions and polydisperse fluorescence centers. This result agrees well with the previous reports [31,32]. In addition, such phenomenon appears also in the upconversion fluorescence and phosphorescence of the nanozyme in the NIR range, but it was not registered in the control experiment. The difference between the excitation energy and the upconversion peak maximum was found to be a constant upon varying the excitation wavelength. When the nanoparticles are suspended in ciscous solvent as glycerol their phosphorescence is clearly visible, because the rate of quencher diffusion is slow. On the other hand, in water the present dissolved oxygen quenches faster the phorphorescence intensity of nanozyme, which is an indication for occuring of photosensitization reaction.

The intracellular uptake and staining of breast cancer cells with fluorescence nanozyme is demonstrated by confocal microscopy images on Fig. 4 . The bright-field contrast image (Fig. 4A) presents the native shapes, morphology and arrangement of untreated cells in a monolayer culture. The uptake and accumulation of nanoparticles in their citoplasm is shown on the epifluorescent image (Fig. 4B) based on the one-photon (down-conversion) fluorescence emission. The further fluorescence microscopic observation revealed irreversible changes related to the overall cellular shapes (Fig. 4C), which are indications for the occurred photo-induced cytotoxic effect caused by the nanozyme. On Fig. 4D is shown an analogous bright-field contrast image of cancer cells which were treated with nanozyme. The cells were further exposed to 700 nm NIR irradiation [34] and two-photon (upconversion) excited fluorescence emission was observed (Fig. 4E). The merged micrograph on Fig. 4F revealed the nanoparticles distribution within the exposed intact cells thanks to the emitted upconversion fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of the nanozyme induced oxygen photosensitization reaction
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the combined XPS and UV–vis absorption data it is obviously that the increased amount of pyrrolic nitrogen centers into the aromatic domains is causing slight blue-shift of the maximum peak from 360 nm to ∼ 350 nm (Fig. 3B). To verify the enhanced oxygen photosensitization performance the nanozyme was illuminated with visible light (400–700 nm, LED, energy dose ∼ 1500 lx) in the presence of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as indicator, Mn(II) ions as mediator of electrons and EDTA as complex agent for stabilization of the formed highly active Mn(III) ions. The oxidation of TMB in just seconds produced intense blue color of the reaction mixture with peak maximum at 645 nm (as shown on Fig. 5 A). It is expected that the photosensitization reaction with oxygen is mainly proceeding in the surface of nanozyme. The conjugated histidine groups are possibly reacting with 1O2 and thus to converted to unstable endoperoxide intermediates and carbonyls as shown on the scheme. This effect has been previously reported [33], and it is demonstrated by slight red shifting of the absorption peak from 350 to 355 nm, which is indication for decreasing the amount of pyridinic surface groups during the reaction. The increased contents of the incorporated histidine residues i.e., pyrrolic N-doping responsible for the higher number of adsorption sites, is matching well with the measured enhance photo-oxidase performance of the nanozyme.

As shown on Fig. 5B, the steady-state catalytic kinetics monitored in time-dependent absorbance changing at 645 nm and room temperature proved the higher rate of oxidation in the case of nanozyme in comparison to those of the histidine-free nanoparticles (as a control experiment). It means that the nanozyme is more powerful to transform triplet to singlet oxygen under irradiation by visible light and the presence of 1O2 can be confirmed from its phosphorescence emission at 1275 nm (Fig. 5C). The highest phosphorescence emission intensity of singlet oxygen was detected in the nanozyme with 40 % histidine-doping, which suggests that the energy from its activated triplet state is transferred to the adsorbed oxygen molecules. Therefore, the contribution of the enhanced pyrrolic nitrogen contents to the nanozyme performance was confirmed again. To further verify its photosensitization ability, an additional characterization by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) was performed. In this experiment, 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) as a trap for 1O2 was employed. A significant amount of the produced singlet oxygen was identified as a typically obsered 1:1:1 triplet signal (Fig. 5D). As we expected, the production of 1O2 is faster at lower pH and therefore, the oxidation of TMB perform faster at acidic conditions too. As result, the EPR data agreed well with the all presented above studies about the nanozyme catalyzed photo-oxidaze reaction. The engineered photosensitization performance of synthesized nanozyme was than utilized for treatment of breast cancer cells and its potential as a theranostics agent for photodynamic therapy application was demonstrated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scratch assay for in vitro testing of melanoma cells migration under normoxia condition
ce:section.ce:para: The in vitro scratch assay experiment was applied to the systematic evaluation of the impact of production of singlet oxygen and induced ROS under normoxia condition on the migration of melanoma cells with highly metastatic potential. To confirm the inhibition effect on the photo-oxidase mimicking nanozyme under visual light irradiation, B16-F10 cancer cells were incubated in the absence (–) or presence (+) of nanoparticles (Fig. 6 ). Both cases were first generating a cell monolayer in a 48-wells microtiter plate. Then, the cell migration was measured by both fluorescence and inverted microscopic observation, through wound healing assay from a same starting state, which was reached immediately after scratching the confluent monolayer on the plate (0 h, upper side micrographic images). As shown on the left-side images as a control experiment i.e., the case where nanozyme was absence (–) demonstrated that after 15 h the adherent cells from both edges bind and closure the gap in almost 65 – 69 % efficacy (left down micrographic image).

However, the number of migratory cells across the wound regions was dramatically decreased after treatment with the oxidase mimicking nanozyme under visible light irradiation (right-side images). The experimental data indicated that the nanoparticles inhibited B16-F10 cell migration, and after 15 h treatment the gap was closure in 5–8 % efficacy (right down micrographic image). These findings suggest the potential anticancer activity of histidine-functionalized carbon nanodots and the possibility to control the tumor cell migration by ROS production. Further, we examined how the dose of the nanozyme i.e., the photo-catalytically generated amount of 1O2 and ROS could affect the efficiency of the inhibited metastatic cell migration in the wounded area. For that purpose, the formed homogeneous cellular monolayer of highly metastatic B16-F10 cancer cells were incubated under normoxia condition with RPMI complete medium and nanozyme with various doses, as follow 0.0 μg/ml (control experiment), 0.5, 5.0, 25.0 and 50 μg/ml, respectively. The culture dishes were illuminated with visible light (LED, 400–700 nm) for 20 min and incubated for 24 h. During that time the cells movement was periodically observed. After reaching of the 24 h post-scratching period the scratch assay was subject of microscopic analysis. For that purpose, the micrographic images were made by phase-contrast microscope in dark field. As shown in Fig. 7 , by increasing the nanozyme doses (from 0 to 50 μg/ml) in the RPMI 1640 complete nutrition medium the inhibition of cell migration was raised in a correlated dose-dependently manner. The experimentally observed cell migration presented in percentage occupied gap area was decreased from 69 to 14 %, respectively for the determined period of testing. Thus, the data obtained revealed that the tumor cells activities under normoxia condition can be controlled effectively by the applied dosage of the designed nanozyme. Therefore, the higher doses decrease the cancer cells proliferation and show acute direct effect on the tumor cell activities and survival. This effect might include cellular membrane damage, as well as the accumulation of higher amount of nanoparticles into cancer cells by mediated endocytosis following by promoting of programmed cell death (apoptosis).

Next, we used MTT assay to evaluate the photo-induced toxicity of nanozyme on melanoma B16-F10 cancer cells. In this experiment the treated cells in the well plates were: (i) not irradiated (0 min irradiation, i.e., control experiment), or (ii) irradiated with white LED light for 10, 20 and 30 min, and then incubated again for 24 h in dark condition. As shown on Fig. 8 , even small difference of the applied nanozyme dose become a sigificant factor for the survival rate of cancer cells, and the dose effect strongly depends also on the irradiation time. The applied doses of nanoparticles were 0.5, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0 25.0 and 50.0 μg/ml, respectively. The occurred photo-induced cytotoxic effect on the rate of cellular survival was measured as a function between the contact irradiation time and the applied dose. The control experiment (0 min irradiation, i.e., dark mode) shows that the viability of cells treated with different nanoparticles doses at dark were not noticeably affected. Nevertheless, the other data demonstrated that the nanozyme upon irradiation increased greatly its photo-induced cytotoxicity in comparison to the control experiment. As expected, the increased doses and the longer irradiation time caused the highest damaging of the cancer cells, as well as decreased rate of B16-F10 cancer cells survival. We concluded that the nanozyme possesses very low cytotoxicity in dark condition, but when irradiated it reacts as an efficient photo-oxidase mimicking enzyme. The obtained MTT assay data are in a good agreement to the previous obtained results from the scratch assay experiment.

In general, the MTT test is used to determine the metabolic activity of the cells [40]. From the test results, we can assume about the photo-cytotoxicity of designed nanozymes. However, increased metabolic activity is not always associated with low cytotoxicity. It is possible that due to external stress (e.g. incubation with various substances) cells can increase their metabolic activity and for this reason a wrong conclusions about cytotoxicity to be made. To avoid such a false suggestion, we determined and plotted a growth curve that directly shows changes in cell number. Additionally, we provided microscopic images to show normal cell proliferation and morphology in untreated cells, and in cells incubated with nanoparticles (His C-dots), which also indicate the absence of cytotoxicity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of the nanozyme on intact mitochondria and submitochondrial particles
ce:section.ce:para: Mitochondria are organelles found in almost all human cells. They are very important for intracellular Ca2+ homeostasis, regulate central metabolic pathways, are the major ATP and ROS producers, and induce apoptosis [41,42]. Mitochondria participate in different neurodegenerations, including toxin and age induced dementia or Alzheimer’s disease and Parkinson’s disease [42,43]. Such destructions or deteriorations of brain cells develop slowly and often is difficult to be evaluated and foreseen by using standard toxicological methods. Additionally, cytotoxicity of nanoparticles was observed due to impairment of the mitochondrial functions via ROS-dependent and ROS-independent mechanisms [44,45]. For all these reasons we studied the effect of the nanozyme on the ATPase activity of intact mitochondria (native, coupled mitochondria) and SMP - closed inverted (inside-out) vesicles, obtained from inner mitochondrial membrane with functionally active ATPases. The possible uncoupling effect of the nanoparticles was studed on intact mitochondria. Fig. 9 A shows the results from experiments with nanozyme (Nz) applied in final doses of 50, 165 and 500 mg/l respectively. ATPase activity remained low during the registration for 300 s under control conditions. The addition of the uncoupler 2,4-dinitrophenol (DNP, 50 µM;) powerfully stimulated ATP hydrolysis. We could not obtain data about the same effect of His C-Dots. This suggests that the nanozyme does not possess an uncoupling effect on intact mitochondria, and it is not able to pass the inner mitochondrial membrane. The barrier of the inner mitochondrial membrane for nanozyme penetration was eliminated in experiments with SMP. As a result, the complex F1 is located on the outside of the membrane and is oriented towards the solution, which allows direct access of the reagent in the medium to the ATPase enzyme. Due to absence of a membrane, some of the properties of ATPase from SMP resemble those of the soluble mitochondrial ATPase (F1). In this case SMP are a suitable model for studying the activity of the membrane-bound mitochondrial ATPase without the limitation of the outer mitochondrial membrane. The results obtained with SMP show an inhibiting effect of nanozyme (Fig. 9B). ATPase activity values were calculated as percentages of the activity (expressed as µmol Pi. mg protein-1.min-1) measured under control conditions (nanoparticle-free assay medium). The ATPase activity of SMP was significantly decreased by nanozyme in doses of 50, 165 and 500 mg/l to 76.46 ± 5.71 % (p = 0.004), 77.68 ± 8.22 % (p = 0.033) and 60.93 ± 8.00 % (p = 0.001) of the control, respectively. This suggests a direct influence of nanozyme on the membrane-bound mitochondrial ATPase.

Data of the effect of the nanozyme on DAO activity of rat liver (left panel) and kidney (right panel) in vitro (A) and in vivo (B) experiments are presented on Fig. 10 . DAO activity values were calculated as percentages of the activity measured under control conditions (nanozyme-free assay media). Application of nanoparticles did not change significantly DAO activity of both studied organs: nanozyme in liver induced moderate decrease of product of DAO reaction (18.7 % ± 2.99; p = 0.116). Similarly, in kidney nanozyme suppressed DAO activity by 13.1 % ± 4.46 % (p = 0.503; panel A). In in vivo experiments the nanoparticles effect in liver was −13.02 % ± 2.43 % (p = 0.216) and in kidney − 9.29 % ± 1.28 % (p = 0.595; panel B). It is concluded that the photo-oxidase nanozyme do not influence significantly DAO activity in the studied common homeostatic organs liver and kidneys.

To shed light on the effect to the cell growth, A549 and HepG2 cells were treated with nanozyme for 6 h and were incubated for seven days (Fig. 11 A, B). Growth curves suggest that there is little or no effect of the nanozyme on A549 and HepG2 cells’ proliferation. Untreated and treated cultures with 350 mg/l or 500 mg/l (data not shown) His C-dots showed gradual increase of cell number and at the 6th day after treatment the cells exhibited uninterrupted monolayers with no sign of apoptotic or necrotic cells.

Morphology and shape of the cells were unaffected (Fig. 12 ). The treated cells did not show any nuclear or cytoplasmic changes. The cytoplasm was homogeneous with no significant alteration in granularity of treated cells. This is in line with our previous studies using fluorescence imaging that these nanoparticles could not penetrate cell membrane (data not shown) and with the results for their non-significant effect on activity of DAO, isolated from rat liver and kidney, and on ATPase activity of native mitochondria in the absence of illumination.

We have designed a fluorescent nanozyme based on histidine-derivative carbon nanodots, which was able to catalyze photosensitization reaction for singlet oxygen production at neutral pH. Its photo-oxidase induced cytotoxicity was demonstrated on highly metastatic breast cancer cells B16-F10 trough inhibition of their migration. Under normoxic condition, the application of increasing nanozyme doses (e.g., up to 50 mg/l) significantly decreased the cell migration from 69 % to 14 % within 24 h post-scratching period. The amount used was 10-fold lower than that of the threshold dose, which was assessed over 500 mg/l through measuring the ATPase activity of intact mitochondria, submitochondrial particles, as well as diamine oxidase activity. All these results suggest the potential application of the reported nanozyme as theranostic agent that could control the tumor cell migration through photodynamic therapy. It is also suggested that His C-dots are non-toxic to non-illuminated cells at doses up to 500 mg/l and do not influence their signal pathways related to cell growth, differentiation and death.

Alexandre Loukanov: Writing – original draft. Mariela Chichova: Methodology. Chavdar Filipov: Methodology. Milena Shkodrova: Methodology. Milena Mishonova: Methodology. Kirilka Mladenova: Data curation. Jordan Doumanov: Data curation. Hristo Gagov: Writing – original draft.
